name: Audrius Merfeldas
full: re) Audrius Merfeldas Kaunas University of Technology lam an EMC consultant and the head of the EMC laboratory at Kaunas University of Technology. I have been working in RF design for over 13 years and hold a PhD in "Development and Investigation of Near-Field Radiated Susceptibility Mapping Methodology for Radio Frequency Devices." Even though many of the topics were familiar to me, it was still very interesting to read through. I have always considered return currents and solid ground polygons beneath coplanar traces, but a valuable point was made about placing additional ground vias near layer transition vias when changing layers from top to bottom. I tend to stitch ground polygons extensively (a habit from my experience in RF), but I hadn’t focused on this aspect before. I truly appreciate the structured and systematic approach to EMC knowledge.

name: Mare Goovaerts ”
full: re) Mare Goovaerts ” Senior Electronic Design Engineer I'm Marc Goovaerts. I have been an electronics design engineer for more than 2 decades in various hardware industries (industrial measurements, power electronics, audio, IOT, radar) and I can honestly say that I already had a firm grasp on all things EMC related. I can nevertheless say that I still learned some valuable things from the free course. The repeatedly mentioned good design practices were already second nature in PCB (and schematic) design for me. But I valued the explanation of CM current creation very high. Similarly, the in depth ESD propagation explanation was highly appreciated too. The theory behind what I already knew about good design practice, will probably prove valuable in debugging badly designed devices under test in the future. Despite all previously acquired knowledge, sometimes EMC design remained confusing, especially on system level when there are more than one PCB, connectors, chassis, safety requirements etc. The gained knowledge from this test will finally and certainly aid in decision making in the future. The hardest part of EMC design will remain politics: speaking with authority to convince peers and management that extra time invested in design for EMC is better and more economical than debugging after failed EMC tests. I'm convinced that there's always something to be learned for the experts and it certainly bridges a big gap between academics and working products for beginners.
